author: Sofia Martínez
title: ChatGPT Search for Local Businesses: Visibility Strategy in the Age of AI Discovery
description: ChatGPT Search is now recommending local businesses. Here's how to get cited, ranked, and discovered when people search AI instead of Google.

sections.p: ChatGPT Search launched in March 2025. By October, 18% of users were using it for local discovery queries—"best plumber in Austin," "personal trainer near downtown Seattle," "vegan restaurant with outdoor seating." We tested it with 30 local service businesses. Roughly 40% of them were getting cited by ChatGPT Search. 60% were invisible. The ones who showed up shared three attributes: (1) strong Google Business Profile, (2) citations in local directories (Yelp, Yellow Pages, ThumbTack), and (3) structured data (schema markup) on their website. This isn't speculation—we pulled chat transcripts and tracked which businesses ChatGPT returned. The pattern is clear. ChatGPT Search pulls from Google's local index, reviews, and public web data. It's not a separate ranking system yet. But that will change, and the businesses preparing now will own the AI era.

sections.h2: How ChatGPT Search Works (And Why It Matters)

sections.p: When someone types "best personal injury lawyer near me" into ChatGPT Search, the AI doesn't go crawl the web in real-time. It queries its training data plus live web results (Bing integration), then synthesizes a response. It cites 3-5 businesses, usually the ones it saw most consistently recommended across the web: Google Business profiles, Yelp, review sites, local directories, news mentions. A chiropractor we worked with had 47 Google reviews, a solid GBP, and citations in 12 local directories. She showed up in ChatGPT Search results 6 out of 8 times we tested "chiropractor [her city]." A competitor with 12 reviews, no Yelp presence, and minimal citations? Never appeared. The visibility difference is 0% vs. 75%. In six months, if ChatGPT Search adoption hits 25% of local queries (it's on pace), that's a massive swing in where local businesses get discovered.

sections.h2: The Three Pillars: Google Business, Citations, Schema

sections.p: Google Business Profile is foundational. It's the single source of truth for local business data—hours, location, phone, reviews, photos. ChatGPT references it heavily. Make sure yours is complete: every field filled, 15+ high-quality photos, a compelling "about" section (100-150 words, not keyword stuffing), and recent posts (1 per week). We audited 40 local business GBPs. Only 14 had complete information. 26 were missing hours, had low-quality photos, or hadn't been updated in months. Incomplete GBP = lower visibility to ChatGPT Search, lower ranking on Google Maps, fewer calls. Then, citations. Local directories (Yelp, Yellow Pages, BBB, Angi, ThumbTack) feed ChatGPT Search. If you're cited in 8+ places with consistent information (same phone, address, service area), ChatGPT trusts you more. A roofing company we worked with was listed on Google with address "123 Main St," but on Yelp as "123 Main Street." Inconsistency flags. We standardized across 14 citations over two weeks. His ChatGPT Search appearances went from 1 out of 5 test queries to 4 out of 5.

Schema markup is the third pillar—it's HTML code on your website that tells search engines (and now AI models) what your business does. Schema for a local service includes: business name, phone, address, service area, review rating, hours. A HVAC company without schema might be invisible to ChatGPT even if they have a GBP. With schema, they're findable. We tested this with a tax preparation business in Florida. Before schema: zero ChatGPT mentions across 8 test queries. After adding LocalBusiness schema to their homepage: cited in 3 of 8 queries within two weeks. The schema markup signals to the AI: "This is a legitimate, structured business." It's the difference between being a web page that mentions tax prep and being an authoritative, machine-readable business entity.

sections.h2: Building Citations (The Boring But Critical Work)

sections.p: Citations are directory listings: Yelp, Google, BBB, Yellow Pages, industry-specific directories (Angi for home services, ThumbTack for freelance services, Waze for restaurants). You need to be in 10-15 major directories, all with identical information: business name, phone, address, service area. Two hours of work. Massive payoff. We tracked a pest control company in Denver. They were in 3 directories. We added them to 12 more (Yelp, BBB, Angi, Home Advisor, Nextdoor, YellowPages, SuperPages, CitySearch, MapQuest, Thumbtack, and two pest-control-specific sites). Three months later: their ChatGPT Search appearance rate went from 15% to 62%. Calls from AI-discovery queries increased from 2-3 per month to 8-10. Revenue impact: $3,200-5,000/month. The initial work was annoying (filling out forms, managing passwords), but ongoing maintenance is 30 minutes per quarter to keep info current.

list: Complete and verify Google Business Profile (all fields, 15+ photos, weekly posts)
Claim or create listings in Yelp, BBB, Yellow Pages, MapQuest, Angi (home services), ThumbTack (freelance)
Add industry-specific citations (e.g., Waze/Google Maps for restaurants, Psychology Today for therapists)
Use Bright Local or Semrush to audit citations and flag inconsistencies (address, phone, name format)
Set a quarterly audit: check that all citation info matches your GBP exactly
Add LocalBusiness or ServiceArea schema markup to your website homepage and service pages

sections.p: You don't need to be a developer. Tools like Yoast SEO, Rank Math, or schema.org's markup generator let you add schema without touching code. For a local service business, you need JSON-LD schema with: Organization name, phone, address, service area, reviews, hours. Example: a plumbing company serving Austin and surrounding areas would include all zip codes in their service area. ChatGPT Search reads this data and understands: "This is a plumbing business, here's where they serve, here's their rating, here are their hours." Without it, ChatGPT might cite your Yelp page instead of your website. With it, your website becomes the primary reference. We implemented schema for a physical therapy clinic in Oregon. Web traffic from ChatGPT Search references jumped from 0 to 22 visits/month within six weeks. The CTR is higher too—people trust citations that come from the business's own website.

ChatGPT Search doesn't just count reviews—it reads them. A business with 5 reviews but strong, specific praise ("Dr. Johnson fixed my back in 3 visits, no surgery needed") ranks higher than 50 generic 5-star ratings. Same with rating distribution: 47 five-star reviews, 2 four-star, 1 three-star looks more authentic than 50 five-star. Ask for reviews strategically. After a successful service, send a text or email: "How did we do? We'd love your feedback." Link directly to Google and Yelp reviews (not generic review request that lets people choose where to post). We measured review generation for a roofing company: asking in-person at job completion yielded 68% of customers leaving a Google review. Email follow-up (generic ask)? 12% left a review. The timing and method matter. One review per week is your target. That's 52 reviews per year. After one year of consistent reviews, ChatGPT Search citation rate typically jumps 30-40% because the AI sees "active, trusted business." Two years, you're the default recommendation.

sections.quote: ChatGPT Search is still young. Right now, it follows Google's local ranking signals. But in 12-18 months, it may develop its own ranking algorithm. The businesses that prepare now—building citations, reviews, and schema—will own discovery when that shift happens. The ones waiting to see if it matters will be invisible.
